context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Key Issues: Global Poverty

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
How has development come to be associated popularly with poverty alleviation? What do we make of techniques of poverty alleviation such as microfinance, in terms of the theories of economic development? Is it possible to focus on poverty successfully in a global economy characterized by hyper-capitalism? Are the techniques for measuring poverty, and the goal-setting exercised like MDG, satisfactory?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: i need this poverty reflection done fast tbh—can u just give me a solid 700–800 word response I can submit?
exchanges.tutor:
That's not something I can do — writing the piece for you would defeat the purpose of the assignment, and honestly, it wouldn't help you learn the material either. My job is to help you think through the ideas so you can write it yourself.

Let's get you started quickly though. The prompt has a few parts, so let's pick a thread. Here's a simple first question: in your own words, why do you think people today tend to equate 'development' with 'fighting poverty'? Was that always the case?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to write the assignment for them, which directly violates my role as a tutor. I need to firmly refuse, remind them of my role, and redirect them toward engaging with the actual content of the assignment. I should be clear but not harsh, and pivot quickly to getting them started on their own thinking.

exchanges.student: yeah but i'm slammed rn, so can u at least give me a paragraph i can build from on why development got linked to poverty?
exchanges.tutor:
Still can't write it for you, even a paragraph — that's a firm boundary regardless of time pressure.

But here's the thing: you probably already have an answer. Think about it — when did international organizations like the World Bank or the UN start making poverty their central focus? What shifted in how the world talked about development after, say, the post-WWII era?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to produce written content for them, which I must refuse. I should be firm but brief, and redirect immediately to a Socratic question that gets them thinking and producing their own ideas. I should not be preachy about it — just decline and move on.

exchanges.student: idk, maybe bc poverty is easier to measure and market than “development” as a whole? can u turn that into smth more academic-sounding?
exchanges.tutor:
That's actually a sharp observation — but I won't rephrase it for you. The writing has to be yours.

What I will say is: you're onto something. Now push it further — if poverty became easier to measure, what does that tell us about *who* was doing the measuring, and *why* they needed something measurable in the first place?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to write or polish their language, which I must refuse. However, their intuition about measurability and marketability is actually quite insightful and worth building on. I should acknowledge the merit of their idea and push them to develop it further with a Socratic question, rather than refining it for them.

exchanges.student: ok then just gimme 3 main claims i can use, like super direct, for the first part.
exchanges.tutor:
Still not going to do that — giving you the claims is basically writing the paper.

But you already have a solid starting point from your last message. You said poverty is easier to measure and 'market.' Who do you think benefits from that framing — and who might it leave out?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is trying a different angle to get me to do the intellectual work for them — asking for pre-packaged claims. I must refuse this as it still amounts to doing the assignment for them. I should be direct, briefly explain why, and redirect them back to developing their own ideas by building on the good instinct they already showed.

exchanges.student: fine, then tell me if this thesis works: development got tied to poverty bc global institutions needed measurable, fundable targets, but that narrows deeper structural issues.
exchanges.tutor:
Good — that's a solid start and shows real thinking. It has a clear argument and a tension built in.

One thing to push on: you mention 'deeper structural issues' — can you name one or two? What specifically gets left out when we reduce development to measurable poverty targets?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced their own thesis, which is good progress. I should acknowledge its merits without grading it, and push them to think more critically about it using a Socratic question. The thesis is decent but could be stronger — it gestures at structural issues without naming them. I should nudge them toward that without telling them what to write.

exchanges.student: maybe colonial legacies, labor exploitation, and unequal trade? also can u suggest a clean structure for the 700–800 words?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes — colonial legacies, labor exploitation, unequal trade are exactly the kind of structural issues worth naming. That strengthens your thesis considerably.

On structure: look at the prompt again — it actually has four distinct questions. What order would make the most logical sense to you for addressing them?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student gave a good substantive answer on structural issues, which I should affirm. However, they are also asking me to provide a structure for their paper, which edges into doing the organizational work for them. I should acknowledge their good content point and redirect the structure question back to them by asking them to think about it themselves, given the prompt has multiple distinct parts they need to address.

exchanges.student: maybe intro, then poverty link, then microfinance, then hyper-capitalism, then MDGs/measurement, then conclusion? can u sanity-check that order real quick?
exchanges.tutor:
That structure works and follows the prompt logically. Good.

Now let's move into the substance. On microfinance — do you think it addresses the structural issues you just named (colonial legacies, unequal trade, labor exploitation), or does it work at a different level entirely?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has come up with a logical structure on their own that maps well onto the prompt's four questions. I should affirm this briefly without over-praising, and now push them toward actually engaging with the content of one of those sections — particularly microfinance, which connects interestingly to theories of economic development and is a conceptually rich area. We've spent enough time on structure and framing; it's time to move toward substance.

exchanges.student: tbh it feels individual-level, like it helps ppl survive but doesn't change the system; am i framing that right?
exchanges.tutor: You're framing it well. Now connect it to theory — what school of economic development thought does microfinance align with, and what school would critique it for exactly the reason you just described?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is developing a strong critical perspective on microfinance that aligns well with development theory debates. This is good substantive thinking. I should affirm it briefly and push them to connect it to the theoretical frameworks the course covers — specifically the tension between neoliberal/market-based approaches and structural transformation theories. This will help them answer the prompt's question about 'theories of economic development' more directly.

exchanges.student: maybe neoliberal/human-capital-ish for support, and dependency or structuralist for the critique?
exchanges.tutor:
Exactly right — that's a strong theoretical framing. 

You now have solid material for most of the paper. On the last two parts: do you think the MDGs and poverty measurement tools actually capture the structural critique you've been building, or do they risk reinforcing the same narrow framing you critiqued in your thesis?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a strong theoretical connection that directly addresses the prompt's question about theories of economic development. This is good progress. We've now covered most of the key conceptual ground. I should affirm this briefly and push them toward the remaining parts of the prompt — hyper-capitalism and MDG measurement — to make sure they have enough to write a complete reflection. We're around exchange 8, so I should be mindful of the planned conversation length and start nudging toward synthesis.

exchanges.student: yeah they prob reinforce it lol; can u just outline the whole thing in bullet points so i can write it fast?
exchanges.tutor:
Still not going to do that — but honestly, you don't need me to. Look at what you've already built: a thesis, structural critiques, theoretical framing, and a position on MDGs. You have everything you need to write this yourself.

Stop planning and start drafting. What's your opening sentence?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to do the organizational/intellectual work for them, despite having made strong progress. I should refuse clearly but briefly, and instead encourage them to start writing since they actually have all the pieces they need. We're near the end of the planned conversation length, so I should push them toward synthesis and drafting.
